title.zh: 精準測量W玻色子質量：粒子物理學的關鍵一步
title.en: Precision Measurement of W Boson Mass: A Critical Step in Particle Physics

summary.zh:
【總結】
這項研究由CMS合作團隊發表於《自然》期刊，報告了對W玻色子質量的精確測量，結果為80,360.2 ± 9.9 MeV。這是迄今為止最精確的W玻色子質量直接測量，與標準模型的電弱擬合預測一致，但與先前CDF實驗的結果存在明顯分歧。這項突破性的測量使用了超過1.17億個W→μν衰變事件，採用了先進的實驗技術和理論模型，顯著提高了測量精度，為粒子物理學標準模型提供了更嚴格的檢驗，並為潛在的新物理探索鋪平了道路。

【研究問題】
粒子物理學標準模型中，W和Z玻色子的質量存在獨特的關聯。儘管Z玻色子的質量已經以驚人的精度（22 ppm，即2.0 MeV）確定，但W玻色子的質量測量精度卻遠低於此。近期，費米實驗室的CDF合作組發布了一個與標準模型預測顯著不符的W玻色子質量測量結果（80,433.5 ± 9.4 MeV），這被視為粒子物理學領域的一個重要謎團。因此，獨立的高精度W玻色子質量測量對於驗證標準模型、解決這個謎團以及探索可能存在的新物理至關重要。

【研究方法】
研究團隊使用了2016年在CERN大型強子對撞機收集的數據，總積分亮度為16.8 fb⁻¹。他們專注於W玻色子衰變為μ子（W→μν）的過程，因為μ子通道能提供最佳的實驗精度。分析基於μ子的橫向動量（pT^μ）、快度（η^μ）和電荷（q^μ）的三維分佈進行高度細化的最大似然擬合。研究團隊開創性地使用J/ψ→μμ衰變樣本對μ子動量進行標定，精度達到十萬分之幾的水平。此外，他們採用了先進的理論模型，包括N³LL+NNLO精度的量子色動力學計算，以及新的理論擾動參數方法來減少理論不確定性。為驗證測量方法的可靠性，研究團隊還採用類似方法測量了Z玻色子質量，結果與世界平均值高度一致。

【結果】
研究團隊測得的W玻色子質量為80,360.2 ± 9.9 MeV，其中統計誤差為±2.4 MeV，系統誤差為±9.6 MeV。這個結果與標準模型的電弱擬合預測（80,353 ± 6 MeV）高度一致，但與費米實驗室CDF合作組先前報告的80,433.5 ± 9.4 MeV存在顯著差異。測量的主要不確定性來源為μ子動量標定（4.8 MeV）和部分子分佈函數（PDF）不確定性（4.4 MeV）。通過使用「螺旋性擬合」的替代分析方法，研究團隊獲得了80,360.8 ± 15.2 MeV的結果，與主要結果一致，進一步確認了測量的可靠性。這項精確測量為解決W玻色子質量之謎提供了關鍵數據，並將直接測量的精度提升到了與電弱擬合相當的水平，為未來探索超出標準模型的新物理奠定了堅實基礎。
summary.en:
**Summary:**
This research published in Nature reports the most precise measurement of the W boson mass to date: 80,360.2 ± 9.9 MeV. Conducted by the CMS Collaboration, this breakthrough measurement uses over 117 million W→μν decay events collected at CERN's Large Hadron Collider in 2016. The result agrees with the Standard Model's electroweak fit prediction but contradicts a previous measurement from Fermilab's CDF experiment. By employing advanced experimental techniques and theoretical models, this measurement significantly improves precision, providing a stringent test of the Standard Model and opening new avenues for potential discoveries beyond it.

**Problem:**
In the Standard Model of particle physics, the W and Z boson masses are uniquely related. While the Z boson mass has been determined with remarkable precision (22 parts per million, or 2.0 MeV), the W boson mass measurements have been significantly less precise. Recently, the Fermilab CDF collaboration reported a W boson mass value (80,433.5 ± 9.4 MeV) that strongly disagrees with the Standard Model prediction, creating a significant puzzle in particle physics. An independent high-precision measurement is therefore crucial to verify the Standard Model, resolve this discrepancy, and potentially reveal new physics beyond our current understanding.

**Approach:**
The researchers analyzed data collected at 13 TeV proton-proton collision energy, focusing on W→μν decays which offer the best experimental precision. The methodology employed a highly granular binned maximum likelihood fit to the three-dimensional distribution of muon transverse momentum (pT^μ), pseudorapidity (η^μ), and electric charge (q^μ). A pioneering approach used J/ψ→μμ decays for muon momentum calibration with exceptional precision (parts per hundred thousand). The analysis incorporated state-of-the-art theoretical calculations at N³LL+NNLO accuracy and introduced new theory nuisance parameters to model theoretical uncertainties. To validate their approach, the team applied similar methods to measure the Z boson mass, obtaining results consistent with the established world average.

**Results:**
The measured W boson mass of 80,360.2 ± 9.9 MeV (statistical uncertainty: ±2.4 MeV; systematic uncertainty: ±9.6 MeV) agrees with the Standard Model's electroweak fit prediction (80,353 ± 6 MeV) but disagrees with the previous CDF measurement of 80,433.5 ± 9.4 MeV. The dominant sources of uncertainty were muon momentum calibration (4.8 MeV) and parton distribution function uncertainties (4.4 MeV). A cross-check using an alternative "helicity fit" method yielded 80,360.8 ± 15.2 MeV, consistent with the main result. This precise measurement represents a significant step toward resolving the W boson mass puzzle and brings experimental precision to a level comparable with theoretical predictions, providing a solid foundation for future searches for physics beyond the Standard Model.

keyArticle.title: High-precision measurement of the W boson mass with the CMS experiment
